course_title: Advanced Writing: Grammar, Syntax and Style
course_description:
This course presents contemporary grammatical and stylistic concepts and strategies that will enhance students' ability to revise and edit their writing.

Welcome to WRIT7260, writers and editors.

Advanced Writing: Grammar, Syntax and Style will introduce you to contemporary models of English grammar and help you apply this explicit knowledge to both writing and editing tasks.

Throughout the semester, we will build on your intuitive understanding of how words work by discovering the form and function of the 9 English word classes and the ways we can combine structural elements to achieve improved clarity – and a variety of stylistic effects.

The lecture materials, tutorial activities, assessment items, and other course resources incorporate a variety of text types and writing styles so you can develop the vocabulary, tools, and confidence to use what you have learnt in a range of writing and editing contexts. We operate according to a #toolsnotrules ethos in which we understand grammar as a series of patterns and relationships – rather than a value judgement – that can enhance the ways we think and talk about writing techniques and editorial decisions.

By participating actively in this course, you will have the opportunity to engage deeply and thoughtfully with the raw materials of written English and sharpen your approaches to writing and editing.

We look forward to learning with you this semester!

CLASS DELIVERY

This course is delivered in face-to-face mode, but it includes some online activities, alongside an abundance of extension material available to you in the Learning Resources folders on Blackboard.

You will get the most out of WRIT7260 if you commit to weekly attendance and participation. Students who come to class week by week typically approach their assessment with greater clarity and confidence – and they develop lasting friendships and professional connections with their classmates.

Please email Amber at a.gwynne@uq.edu.au if you would like to discuss any aspect of this course, including whatever access requirements and adjustments we can make to help ensure you get the most out of your study.

course_aims: This course aims to build on students’ intuitive understanding of how English words and phrases work to improve their competence and confidence when writing, editing, and analysing text.

learning_outcomes.description: confidently identify grammatical features and patterns of contemporary written English
recognise grammatical choices that affect clarity, cohesion, and style
use your explicit knowledge of English grammar to reflect on and revise a variety of texts at both the structural and functional levels
integrate grammatical conventions to adapt, refine and polish writing for specific audiences, genres, and purposes.

assessment_summary.title: Supervised tutorial problem set – structural and stylistic analysis

assessment_details.title: 7x online quizzes
assessment_details.task_description: The online quizzes test how well you have understood the concepts covered in readings, lectures, and classes throughout the semester and how well you can recognise particular structures, written conventions, and potential errors. You will complete the 7 quizzes on Blackboard for a maximum total of 30% of your overall course grade. In each quiz, you will answer 10 randomised questions worth half a mark each for a total of 5% per quiz. At the end of semester, your lowest mark will be dropped, giving you a maximum total out of 30% based on your 6 highest quiz attempts. Each quiz will open the same day as your lecture and remain open for 7 days, closing at 4.00 pm the following week. The quizzes are open book. You may refer to your readings, lecture notes, and tutorial activities – as well as any other print or online resources that may help you. However, you should use authoritative sources and work independently. You must not share your answers with classmates until after the quiz closes. Make sure you are well prepared and have everything you need in front of you before you begin each quiz. The quiz questions will be based on the following areas: Quiz 1 – Word choices for readers (due 12 August) Quiz 2 – Nouns & determiners (due 19 August) Quiz 3 – Pronouns & adjectives (due 26 Aug) Quiz 4 – Verbs & adverbs (due 9 September) Quiz 5 – Function words (due 16 September) Quiz 6 – Clauses & sentences (due 23 September) Quiz 7 – Punctuation basics (due 28 October) Please note: This is a class assessment item, not an assignment or exam, and you are not able to apply for an extension via the UniTask portal. If you are unwell or experiencing other disruptive circumstances, please contact your course coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit Online via Blackboard.
Blackboard Assignments
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Important submission information
You must click on the Submit button to submit your work for assessment. Academic staff will not see the files if you have merely saved them as a submission in progress. Files that are submitted CANNOT be retrieved for editing and re-submission. Once submitted, they are delivered to the Course Coordinator. It is your responsibility to check the Submission History and confirm your assignment was successfully submitted. To allow for the many possible technical problems with computers and the internet, students are advised to commence assignment uploads at least 3 hours before they are due. If you don’t receive a submission ID, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
· Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot).
· Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again.
· If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Contact your course coordinator in the event of illness or other extenuating circumstances.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Supervised tutorial problem set – structural and stylistic analysis
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This class-based activity gives you an opportunity to apply your developing knowledge of English structure and style to a sample of published text. In your perusal time, you will annotate a passage of text, reflecting on some of the ways the author has used words, phrases, and clauses (forms and structures) to achieve a particular style or effect. You will discuss your responses with classmates and consider what you can learn from the example to enhance your own writing and editing strategies. The written component of this task then involves answering 3 questions about the text in paragraph form. The reflection is worth 30% of your overall course grade. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students are NOT able to apply for an extension via the Unitask portal for this assessment item. Please contact your course coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available.

assessment_details.title: Editing task
assessment_details.task_description: The final editing task gives you an opportunity to apply what you have learnt throughout the semester to an activity you might encounter in the workplace (or as a professional editor): marking up a passage of text with corrections and advice to the author. This task is based loosely on the IPEd test for editors seeking accreditation in Australia. It's an excellent opportunity to gauge your current skill and accuracy as a writing and editing professional. The task comprises 10 sequential blocks of text that contain a total of 30 errors or obvious weaknesses. Your answer sheet will provide space for you to identify and describe each error or problem (for example, ‘spelling error—spell as X instead’ or ‘run-on sentence—insert semicolon’). Each note is worth one mark for a total of 30/40 marks. Your answer sheet will also provide space for you to re-write the entire passage to eliminate errors and improve clarity, tone, and structure, for a total of 10/40 marks. To submit your assignment, you should upload your completed answer sheet and submit it via Turnitin. Please make sure your answers are as specific and coherent as possible. (Remember: imagine you are describing the problem to a client.) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit online
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Welcome to WRIT7260 Course overview, objectives, and assessment structure Brief history of English grammar and its evolution Why grammar matters for professional writers and editors Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 2: Writing for readers This week introduces you to the principles of audience awareness and context sensitivity. It addresses the importance of word choice not only to convey meaning but also a particular persona, tone, style, and effect. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 2: Making confident word choices In this tutorial, you'll complete some hands-on activities to introduce you to English word classes and different categories of words to consider when you're choosing familiar, specific, and appropriate terminology for your readers. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 3: Determiners and nouns – the sentence subject This week introduces you to the subject position of an English sentence by considering nouns and determiners. We’ll cover: Notional vs formal characteristics of nouns and determiners Noun forms and the sentence subject position/function Common, proper, concrete, abstract, collective, and compound nouns Count vs non-count nouns and their patterns of use Determiners (articles, demonstratives, possessives, quantifiers) How noun choices can impact tone, precision, and clarity To prepare for your classes, read The Writer's Diet — ‘Noun density’. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 3: Using nouns effectively In this tutorial, you'll complete some hands-on activities to help you better understand sentence subjects in English and the structure of a noun phrase. You'll practise identifying nouns in context, describing the way an author might use them to achieve different effects, and some standard conventions to master as an editor. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 4: Pronouns and adjectives – the sentence object This week introduces you to the object position of an English sentence by considering pronouns and adjectives. We’ll cover: SVO word order Notional vs formal characteristics of pronouns and adjectives Personal, possessive, reflexive, relative, and indefinite pronouns Pronoun reference, agreement, and case Attributive vs predicative adjectives Degrees of comparison and adjective ordering Creating precision using pronouns and adjectives To prepare for your classes, read The Writer's Diet — ‘Waste words’. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 4: Using pronouns and adjectives In this tutorial, you'll complete some hands-on activities to help you integrate pronouns and adjectives into your writing. You'll practise identifying pronouns and adjectives in context, describing the way an author might use these parts of speech to add colour and precision to their writing, and some standard conventions to master as an editor. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 5: Verbs – the sentence predicate This week introduces you to the heart of an English sentence – the finite verb or predicate. We’ll cover: Notional vs formal characteristics of verbs Finite verbs as the core of a sentence predicate Subject–verb agreement Main vs auxiliary verbs Verb tense and aspect Regular vs irregular verb forms Transitive, intransitive, and linking verbs Phrasal or multi-word verbs To prepare for your classes, read The Writer's Diet — ‘Verbal verve’. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 5: Using verbs In this tutorial, you'll complete some hands-on activities to help you integrate verbs into your writing. You'll practise identifying finite and non-finite verbs in context, as well as understanding the structure of a verb phrase, describing the way an author might manipulate verb tense and aspect in their writing, and applying standard conventions as an editor. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 6: Verbs and verbals – continued This week continues our journey through English verbs, the most pivotal part of an English sentence. We’ll cover: Active vs passive voice Non-finite verb forms and their surprising functions Adverb types and functions Adverb placement and its effect on clarity/meaning Using adverbs wisely 3 common modifier problems and how to avoid them To prepare for your classes, read The Writer's Diet — ‘Ad-dictions’. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 6: Understanding voice and using non-finite verb forms In this tutorial, you'll consider the communicative options available to you when choosing between active and passive voice. You'll practise identifying non-finite verb forms in context and the ways they add extra action, dynamism, and dimension to our sentences. You'll also consider the ways authors manipulate verb phrases in their writing – and some additional conventions to be aware of as an editor. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 7: Connections and relationships – English function words This week, we’re expanding our sentences by considering two function words that establish relationships. We’ll cover: Preposition types and functions, including collocations Common preposition problems and areas of confusion Coordinating, subordinating, and correlative conjunctions Conjunctive adverbs How prepositions and conjunctions affect sentence flow To prepare for your classes, read The Writer's Diet — ‘Prepositional podge’. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 7: Using function words In this tutorial, you'll practise expanding and combining clauses by using prepositional phrases and coordinating/subordinating conjunctions. You'll work on identifying different sentence types and consider how authors play with sentence structure to achieve a variety of semantic and aesthetic effects in their writing. You'll also practise editing writing at the sentence level to resolve common errors and weaknesses. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 8: Clauses and sentences This week, we’re putting our learning together to explore clauses and the building blocks of larger sentences. We’ll cover: Independent and dependent clauses Restrictive vs non-restrictive clauses Simple, compound, complex, and compound-complex sentences Sentence weight and branching Creating sentence variety To prepare for your classes, read ThoughtCo ‘Sentence Definition and Examples in English Grammar’ (Bb). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 8: Writing sentences Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 10: Tutorial problem set In this tutorial, you'll complete your in-class assessment activity: analysing, discussing, and responding to a passage of text. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11: Sentences in sequence This week, we’re looking at ways to refine and combine sentences, exploring what makes a text clear, coherent, and cohesive. We’ll cover: Common sentence-level errors in carefully edited text Natural paragraph variation across text types Achieving clarity, coherence, and cohesion at the macro level Elements of style To prepare for your classes, start reading The Briefest Grammar and Punctuation Guide Ever! Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11: Writing and editing at the paragraph level In this tutorial, you'll sharpen your writing and editing skills by considering coherence and cohesion when combining sentences. You'll then apply your knowledge of common sentence-level errors to refine passages of text for different audiences and purposes. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 12: Words on the page This week, we’re looking at punctuation and formatting. We’ll cover: Punctuation basics for professional writers and editors Accessibility conventions in contemporary edited English Principles of sound document design To prepare for your classes, finish reading The Briefest Grammar and Punctuation Guide Ever! Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 12: Copyediting to strengthen punctuation In this tutorial, you'll get some hands-on experience editing passages of text to make sure their punctuation is accurate, consistent, and elegant. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 13: Course reflection and advanced stylistic techniques In this final lecture, we'll reflect on our learning this semester and consider the value of explicit knowledge of grammar as a part of our writing and editing toolkits. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 13: Course reflection and editing workshop In this tutorial, you'll look back over your semester of learning and work through an editing task, to prepare you for your final assessment item in the course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however, please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
